title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee March 17-18, 2026
summary: The March 17-18, 2026 FOMC meeting occurred amid significant market turbulence driven by AI-related business model concerns and a Middle East conflict that caused crude oil front-month futures to rise approximately 50 percent. Market pricing shifted notably toward a higher-for-longer stance, with rate cuts not fully priced in until December and approximately 30 percent probability of rate hikes through early next year, though the Desk Survey still showed two 25bp cuts expected. The Committee assessed that real GDP continued expanding at a solid pace, the labor market remained resilient with low job gains, and PCE inflation stood at 2.8 percent in January.
key_points: Crude oil front-month futures increased approximately 50 percent over the intermeeting period due to Middle East conflict, with longer-dated futures rising significantly less, suggesting markets viewed the price spike as temporary
Near-term federal funds rate path implied by futures shifted higher, with a rate cut not fully priced in until December; modal path from options consistent with no rate change this year
Options-implied probability of rate hikes through early next year increased to approximately 30 percent, with distribution becoming more dispersed
One-year inflation swap rate rose nearly 50 basis points, but forward measures of inflation compensation beyond one year were little changed
Standing repo operations reached $30 billion on one day (third-largest volume since inception), suggesting greater willingness to use operations following December changes
Treasury yields ended higher, more so at short end, with changes in term premiums accounting for substantial fraction of yield changes
Several central banks previously expected to remain on hold or ease (ECB, BoC, SNB) were now expected to hike rates modestly this year due to energy price-driven global inflationary pressures
Broad equity prices fell approximately 5 percent over the period, with software sector underperforming due to AI-related concerns
Reserves projected to average approximately $3 trillion through September, with trough expected in late April around year-end levels
PCE inflation was 2.8 percent in January; total consumer price inflation remained elevated
why_it_matters: These minutes provide a comprehensive snapshot of FOMC deliberations during a period of heightened uncertainty. The shift in market pricing toward fewer rate cuts and elevated hike probabilities reflects genuine uncertainty about the inflation trajectory amid energy price shocks. The Committee's assessment that oil price increases may be transitory (supported by the flattening of the futures curve) is critical for determining whether recent price pressures warrant a policy response. Additionally, the improved utilization of standing repo facilities indicates evolving market structure that affects how the Fed manages overnight liquidity.
macro_context: The March 2026 FOMC meeting occurred with the U.S. economy showing solid GDP growth after adjusting for Q4 government shutdown effects, low but continued job gains, and elevated PCE inflation at 2.8 percent. The Middle East conflict introduced energy price shock that repriced near-term inflation expectations higher (one-year inflation swap +50bp) while longer-term expectations remained anchored. This creates a complex policy calculus where temporary supply shocks may require careful distinction from persistent inflation dynamics. The Committee faces a situation where financial conditions tightened significantly (equities -5%, yields higher) and global central banks may pivot to tightening, complicating U.S. policy path.
market_structure_context: Treasury market liquidity diminished somewhat with increased yield volatility but markets continued to function well. Money market conditions remained stable with effective federal funds rate 1bp below IORB and repo rates near IORB. Standing repo operations saw increased usage ($30bn on peak settlement day), with the manager interpreting this as greater willingness to use facilities following December changes. RMPs continue, with reserves expected at trough of ~year-end levels in late April before averaging ~$3 trillion through September. Monthly RMP pace likely to be reduced significantly after April as nonreserve liability swings moderate.
important_numbers: 50 basis points (one-year inflation swap rate rise)
30 percent (options-implied probability of rate hikes through early next year)
